behaviors.description: The generated function signature matches the expected interface (parameter names, types, return type) specified in the description.
behaviors.justification: The function signature is generated entirely by the LLM. Real LLM outputs are non-deterministic: across multiple runs with the same prompt, it may generate different parameter names, omit type hints, or produce wrong return types. A mock always returns consistent, correct signatures, hiding the real failure mode (LLM producing mismatched signatures). Only a real LLM call captures whether the service reliably extracts correct signatures from actual, varied LLM outputs.
behaviors.test_description: Call the service 10+ times with a real LLM, providing a natural language description that specifies an exact function signature (e.g., 'function foo(x: int, y: str) -> bool'). Extract the generated function signature from each run's output and verify it matches the spec: same parameter names, types, and return type. Track success rate across runs to confirm the LLM reliably matches the interface spec. This captures real LLM non-determinism in signature generation.

behaviors.description: The generated implementation passes all provided test cases on the first or a subsequent attempt.
behaviors.justification: The behavior explicitly covers both 'first attempt' (single LLM call, Agentic) and 'subsequent attempt' (refinement loop, Workflow). To test the refinement path, the real LLM must sometimes fail initial tests (non-deterministic), triggering the loop. Across multiple runs, some will pass on first try, others will require refinement. A deterministic mock cannot reproduce this variability. The full loop—generate → test → refine if needed → test again—depends on real LLM behavior at each step. Minimum level is Workflow to cover both paths.
behaviors.test_description: For a moderately complex function (one where the real LLM has ~50-70% first-attempt success rate), call the service 15+ times with a real LLM. For each run, record whether tests pass on the first attempt or require refinement loops. Verify that across all runs, the service eventually produces passing code (either immediately or after refinement). Document the distribution: ~X% first-attempt success, ~Y% fixed by refinement, 0% still failing. This validates the full loop works and captures real LLM non-determinism in both paths.

behaviors.description: When tests fail, the refinement step includes the failure messages in the next LLM prompt so the model can learn from its mistakes.
behaviors.justification: This behavior tests prompt construction: when tests fail, the failure output must be included in the subsequent LLM call's prompt. This is deterministic and testable without a real LLM. Mock the LLM so the first call returns code that fails all provided tests; capture the second LLM call's prompt (via mock spy/assertion) and verify it includes the failure message. The behavior is about whether the message is *included* in the prompt, not whether the LLM uses it effectively (that is Behavior 5's concern). No non-determinism is needed; we control the first failure.
behaviors.test_description: Mock the LLM so the first generated code fails all provided test cases. Run the service with a real test suite; capture the test failure output. Spy on the second LLM call's prompt input and verify it includes the failure message (e.g., assertion on mock.call_args). Repeat with different test suites and failure messages to confirm the pattern. This is a deterministic test of prompt construction logic, not LLM behavior.

behaviors.description: The service stops after 3 failed attempts and returns a structured error with all three attempts and their test results.
behaviors.justification: This is a deterministic loop-control behavior. Mock the LLM to always return code that fails all tests. Run the service and verify it makes exactly 3 LLM calls, runs tests 3 times, and exits with a structured error containing all 3 attempts' results. The test does not depend on real LLM non-determinism; we control failure outcomes via the mock. We are testing that the counter/loop limit works correctly, not LLM behavior.
behaviors.test_description: Mock the LLM to return code that fails all provided tests on every call. Run the service. Verify the service makes exactly 3 LLM calls (no more, no fewer), executes the test suite 3 times, and returns a structured error object containing all 3 attempts with their test failure results. Assert that it does not make a 4th call. Verify the error structure includes attempt numbers, generated code snippets, and test output for each attempt.

behaviors.description: The full loop — generate, test, refine, test again — produces a working implementation for a function that a single LLM call typically gets wrong.
behaviors.justification: The phrase 'a function that a single LLM call typically gets wrong' is crucial. This requires identifying a function where the real LLM has <50% first-attempt success rate, then demonstrating the refinement loop improves the outcome. Only a real LLM can establish that difficulty baseline. Mocking to fail-then-pass does not test real refinement behavior; it fabricates artificial difficulty. The loop is multi-step (generate → test → refine → test), and success depends on whether the real LLM actually learns from failure feedback across iterations.
behaviors.test_description: First, baseline: run 15+ raw LLM calls on a moderately complex function (no refinement loop) and confirm <50% first-attempt success. Then, run the service's full refinement loop 15+ times on the same function. Verify that the loop achieves significantly higher success rate (target >80%), proving that refinement adds value for functions the LLM initially struggles with. Document: baseline success rate vs. loop success rate. This demonstrates the loop works for hard cases where single LLM calls fail.
